items.html:
Awareness is not the same as being qualified

This course raises your awareness of first aid so that you can recognise an emergency and act sensibly while help is on its way. Please be clear: this is awareness only and does not make you a qualified first aider. Real competence in first aid needs practical, hands-on training and assessment with a qualified trainer — something a screen cannot give you.

If you may be the designated first aider in your workplace, you need a recognised, assessed course such as Emergency First Aid at Work. In any life-threatening emergency, call 999 straight away and do only what you can safely do until the ambulance service arrives.

First aid kits, provision and hygiene

Your employer must make suitable first aid provision. In a care setting that usually means a stocked first aid kit, an appointed person or trained first aider, and clear information on what to do in an emergency.

  • Know your kit: find out where it is kept, and check that dressings, plasters and gloves are in date and restocked after use.
  • Protect against infection: wash your hands and put on disposable gloves before giving first aid whenever you can.
  • Guard against blood: treat blood and body fluids as a possible source of infection and avoid direct contact.
  • Clean up safely: dispose of used dressings and gloves as clinical waste and wash your hands afterwards.

items.question: A person you support has a bleeding wound and you are about to help. What is the best first step for hygiene and infection control?

answers.text: Put on disposable gloves, and wash your hands first if you have time.
answers.feedback: Correct. Gloves and clean hands protect both you and the person from infection when there is contact with blood.

answers.text: Use your bare hands so that you can feel the wound more clearly.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Bare-handed contact with blood is an infection risk; you should wear disposable gloves whenever you can.

answers.text: Skip the gloves because this is someone you know well.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. How well you know someone makes no difference to the infection risk from blood and body fluids.

answers.text: Only rinse your hands afterwards, as putting on gloves wastes time.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Gloves take only moments and are a basic infection-control measure that should not be skipped.

items.html:
Head and spinal injuries

A blow to the head can range from a mild bump to a serious brain injury. Watch for warning signs that develop after a knock to the head: drowsiness, confusion, repeated vomiting, a severe headache, unequal pupils, or fluid or blood coming from the nose or ears. These are red flags — call 999.

If someone has fallen from a height, had a heavy fall, or has neck or back pain after an accident, suspect a spinal injury. Unnecessary movement could cause lasting harm, so encourage them to keep still and gently support their head in the position you found it while you wait for help.

Broken bones, sprains and strains

You cannot always tell a fracture from a sprain, so if you are in any doubt, treat it as the more serious injury. Signs of a broken bone include pain, swelling, bruising, an odd shape, and being unable to use the limb.

  • Keep it still: support the injured part in the position that is most comfortable and avoid moving it unnecessarily.
  • Do not move the person needlessly: only move someone who is in danger, and never force a limb straight.
  • Cool a sprain: a cold pack wrapped in a cloth can ease pain and swelling in a soft-tissue injury.
  • Get the right help: call 999 for an open or severe fracture, and arrange other medical care for lesser injuries.

items.question: If someone may have a spinal injury after a fall, you should quickly move them into a more comfortable position to help them relax.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Unnecessary movement can worsen a spinal injury, so keep the person still, support their head in the position found, and wait for the ambulance.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false: moving someone with a suspected spinal injury can cause lasting harm, so keep them still and support the head until help arrives.

items.html:
Recognising a seizure

Epilepsy is a common condition, and a person may also have a seizure for other reasons, such as a very high temperature or low blood sugar. In a generalised seizure, someone may suddenly fall, go stiff and then make jerking movements, may not respond to you, and may lose control of their bladder or bowel.

A seizure can look frightening, but most stop on their own within a few minutes. Your job is not to stop the seizure but to keep the person safe while it runs its course and to know when extra help is needed.

What to do and what not to do

Stay calm and protect the person from harm.

  • Protect them: clear the space around them, cushion their head, and move away anything hard or sharp.
  • Note the time: time how long the seizure lasts, as this affects whether you call 999.
  • Do not restrain: never hold them down, and never put anything in their mouth.
  • Afterwards: once the jerking stops, place them in the recovery position and reassure them gently as they come round.

Call 999 if a seizure lasts more than five minutes, if one seizure follows another without recovery, if it is their first ever seizure, or if they are injured or do not wake up.

items.question: Someone is having a seizure on the floor, with jerking movements of their arms and legs. What should you do?

answers.text: Move objects away, cushion their head, and let the seizure run its course while timing it.
answers.feedback: Correct. Protecting them from injury and timing the seizure is safe practice; you only intervene further if it lasts too long or they are hurt.

answers.text: Hold their arms and legs firmly to stop the shaking.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You should never restrain someone during a seizure, as it can cause injury; let the movements happen safely.

answers.text: Put a spoon or your fingers in their mouth so they cannot bite their tongue.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Never put anything in the mouth of someone having a seizure — it can cause serious injury to them or to you.

answers.text: Lift them onto a nearby chair so that they are more comfortable.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Moving someone mid-seizure risks injury; keep them where they are, cushion the head and clear the area.

items.html:
Severe allergic reaction: anaphylaxis

Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can develop within minutes of contact with a trigger, such as certain foods, insect stings or medicines. Signs include a widespread rash, swelling of the face, lips, tongue or throat, difficulty or noisy breathing, a sense of something being seriously wrong, and collapse.

Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency. Call 999 immediately and tell the call handler that you suspect anaphylaxis, so that the right help is sent quickly.

Adrenaline auto-injectors and asthma attacks

Many people at risk of anaphylaxis carry an adrenaline auto-injector, such as an EpiPen. If the person has one, help them use it without delay, following the instructions on the device, and note the time. A second dose may be given after five minutes if there is no improvement and another injector is available.

  • Position them: help them sit up if breathing is hard, or lie flat with legs raised if they feel faint.
  • Asthma attack: help the person sit up and take their reliever inhaler, usually blue, one puff at a time.
  • Call 999: if the inhaler does not help, symptoms get worse, or they cannot speak in full sentences.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on severe allergic reactions and asthma attacks.
items.textField: A severe, life-threatening allergic reaction is called *anaphylaxis*. If you suspect it, call *999* straight away. If the person carries an adrenaline *auto-injector*, help them use it without delay. Someone having an asthma attack should sit up and use their reliever inhaler, which is usually *blue*. A second dose of adrenaline may be given after *five* minutes if there is no improvement.

items.html:
Stroke and heart attack

A stroke happens when the blood supply to part of the brain is cut off, and fast action helps save brain tissue. Remember FAST: Face — has it dropped on one side?; Arms — can they raise both and keep them there?; Speech — is it slurred or muddled?; Time — if you see any one of these signs, it is time to call 999.

A heart attack may cause central chest pain that can spread to the arms, neck or jaw, along with breathlessness, sweating and nausea. Call 999, help the person rest in a comfortable position, and if they are not allergic and it is to hand, offer a single 300mg aspirin to chew slowly.

Fainting, and a real scenario

Fainting is a brief loss of consciousness caused by a temporary drop in blood flow to the brain, and the person usually recovers quickly. If someone feels faint, help them lie down and raise their legs; as they come round, reassure them and let them get up slowly.

Scenario. During a lunch club, Mr Devlin suddenly cannot lift his right arm, the right side of his face has drooped, and his words are slurred. He is still awake. You recognise these as possible signs of a stroke, note the time the symptoms began, call 999, and stay with him, keeping him calm and comfortable until the ambulance arrives.

items.question: At a lunch club, Mr Devlin's face has drooped on one side, he cannot raise his right arm, and his speech is slurred. What should you do?

answers.text: Recognise the FAST signs of a stroke, call 999 immediately, and note when the symptoms started.
answers.feedback: Correct. Face, Arms and Speech point to a stroke, and any one FAST sign means it is time to call 999; the onset time helps the hospital.

answers.text: Give him a cup of tea and a biscuit and see if he improves after his meal.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A stroke needs urgent hospital treatment; delaying to see if he improves wastes vital time and could cause lasting harm.

answers.text: Help him walk around to get his circulation going.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Walking him around does not treat a stroke and risks a fall; the priority is to call 999 without delay.

answers.text: Wait an hour to see whether the symptoms pass before calling anyone.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. With a stroke, the faster the response the better the outcome, so you call 999 as soon as you spot a FAST sign.
